description.example:
Adversaries may deliver payloads to remote systems by adding content to shared storage locations, such as network drives or internal code repositories. Content stored on network drives or in other shared locations may be tainted by adding malicious programs, scripts, or exploit code to otherwise valid files. Once a user opens the shared tainted content, the malicious portion can be executed to run the adversary's code on a remote system. Adversaries may use tainted shared content to move laterally.

A directory share pivot is a variation on this technique that uses several other techniques to propagate malware when users access a shared network directory. It uses [Shortcut Modification](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1547/009) of directory .LNK files that use [Masquerading](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1036) to look like the real directories, which are hidden through [Hidden Files and Directories](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1564/001). The malicious .LNK-based directories have an embedded command that executes the hidden malware file in the directory and then opens the real intended directory so that the user's expected action still occurs. When used with frequently used network directories, the technique may result in frequent reinfections and broad access to systems and potentially to new and higher privileged accounts. (Citation: Retwin Directory Share Pivot)

Adversaries may also compromise shared network directories through binary infections by appending or prepending its code to the healthy binary on the shared network directory. The malware may modify the original entry point (OEP) of the healthy binary to ensure that it is executed before the legitimate code. The infection could continue to spread via the newly infected file when it is executed by a remote system. These infections may target both binary and non-binary formats that end with extensions including, but not limited to, .EXE, .DLL, .SCR, .BAT, and/or .VBS.

detection.example:
Processes that write or overwrite many files to a network shared directory may be suspicious. Monitor processes that are executed from removable media for malicious or abnormal activity such as network connections due to Command and Control and possible network Discovery techniques.

Frequently scan shared network directories for malicious files, hidden files, .LNK files, and other file types that may not typical exist in directories used to share specific types of content.

description.example:
Protect shared folders by minimizing users who have write access. Use utilities that detect or mitigate common features used in exploitation, such as the Microsoft Enhanced Mitigation Experience Toolkit (EMET).

Reduce potential lateral movement risk by using web-based document management and collaboration services that do not use network file and directory sharing.

Identify potentially malicious software that may be used to taint content or may result from it and audit and/or block the unknown programs by using whitelisting (Citation: Beechey 2010) tools, like AppLocker, (Citation: Windows Commands JPCERT) (Citation: NSA MS AppLocker) or Software Restriction Policies (Citation: Corio 2008) where appropriate. (Citation: TechNet Applocker vs SRP)

Identify potentially malicious software that may be used to taint content or may result from it and audit and/or block the unknown programs by using whitelisting (Citation: Beechey 2010) tools, like AppLocker, (Citation: Windows Commands JPCERT) (Citation: NSA MS AppLocker) or Software Restriction Policies (Citation: Corio 2008) where appropriate. (Citation: TechNet Applocker vs SRP)

description.example: An adversary may perform shell escapes or exploit vulnerabilities in an application with the setsuid or setgid bits to get code running in a different users context. On Linux or macOS, when the setuid or setgid bits are set for an application, the application will run with the privileges of the owning user or group respectively. (Citation: setuid man page). Normally an application is run in the current users context, regardless of which user or group owns the application. However, there are instances where programs need to be executed in an elevated context to function properly, but the user running them doesnt need the elevated privileges. Instead of creating an entry in the sudoers file, which must be done by root, any user can specify the setuid or setgid flag to be set for their own applications. These bits are indicated with an "s" instead of an "x" when viewing a file's attributes via ls -l. The chmod program can set these bits with via bitmasking, chmod 4777 [file] or via shorthand naming, chmod u+s [file]. Adversaries can use this mechanism on their own malware to make sure they're able to execute in elevated contexts in the future.(Citation: OSX Keydnap malware).

detection.example: Monitor the file system for files that have the setuid or setgid bits set. Monitor for execution of utilities, like chmod, and their command-line arguments to look for setuid or setguid bits being set.

parameters.name: version
parameters.description:
Pass the Saved Result Set version. 
Allowed values: v2, v3.
Default value: v2
v2: Retrieves the data that is published using the Save Result Set action in rules. Saved Result Set v2 retrieves indicator and vulnerability data only.
v3: Retrieves the data that is published using the Save Result Set V3 action in rules.

parameters.description: Pass the page number to retrieve records. Default value: 1

get.description:
Retrieves a list of rules from the platform.

#### Request Parameters

This API endpoint does not require any input parameter.

#### **Response Parameters**

| **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| next | String | Returns a link to the next page of the response. |
| page_size | Integer | Returns the number of rules retrieved per page. |
| previous | String | Returns a link to the previous page of the response. |
| total | Integer | Returns the total number of rules available in the platform. |
| id | String | Returns the rule ID. |
| name | String | Returns the name of the rule. |
| source | Array | Returns the name of the sources of the rule. |
| is_active | Boolean | Returns true if the rule is in active or draft state, else returns false. |
| last_activity_on | Timestamp | Returns the date and time at which the rule was last executed successfully. |
| ctix_created | Timestamp | Returns the date and time at which the rule was created. |
| ctix_modified | Timestamp | Returns the date and time at which the rule was last modified. |
| is_follow | Boolean | Returns true if the current user follows the rule, else returns false. |
| no_conditions | Boolean | Returns true if the rule is configured with no conditions, else returns false. |
| all_sources_and_collections | Boolean | Returns true if the rule is configured with all sources, else returns false. |
| status | String | Returns the status of the rule. |
| tags | Array | Returns the list of tags applied on a rule. |
| created_by | JSON Object | Returns the details of the user who created the rule. |
| updated_by | JSON Object | Returns the details of the user who last modified the rule. |
| trigger_on_update | Boolean | Returns true if the rule is configured to trigger on an update, else returns false. |

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Pass the page number to retrieve rules.

parameters.name: page_size
parameters.description: Pass the number of rules to retrieve per page.

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: Pass a comma-separated list of source IDs to filter rules.

parameters.name: created_by_id
parameters.description: Pass the ID of the creator of the rules to filter.

parameters.name: status
parameters.description: Pass the status of the rules. Allowed values: DRAFT, ACTIVE, INACTIVE. By default, rules with all status are retrieved.

parameters.name: last_active_to
parameters.description: Pass the last active time in EPOCH format to filter rules.

parameters.name: last_active_from
parameters.description: Pass the last active time in EPOCH format to filter rules.

parameters.name: created_from
parameters.description: Pass the creation time in EPOCH format to filter rules.

parameters.name: created_to
parameters.description: Pass the creation time in EPOCH format to filter rules.

parameters.name: is_manual_run
parameters.description: Pass true to retrieve rules that are configured for manual execution only.

parameters.name: nominal
parameters.description: Pass true to retrieve the ID and title of the rules only.

get.description:
Retrieves the details of a rule.

#### Request Parameters

This API endpoint does not require any input parameter.

#### **Response Parameters**

| **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| id | String | Returns the ID of the rule. |
| name | String | Returns the name of the rule. |
| description | String | Returns the description of the rule. |
| actions | Array | Returns the list of actions applied to the rule. |
| rule | Array | Returns the list of conditions applied to the rule. |
| rule_source | Array | Returns the list of source objects of the rule. |
| no_conditions | Boolean | Returns true if no conditions are configured on the rule, else returns false. |
| is_active | Boolean | Returns true if the rule is active, else returns false. |
| status | String | Returns the status of the rule as Active, Inactive, or Draft. |
| tags | Array | Returns the list of tags applied to the rule. |
| created_by | String | Returns the ID of the user who created the rule. |
| updated_by | String | Returns the ID of the user who last modified the rule. |
| trigger_on_update | Boolean | Returns true if a rule is configured to trigger on an update, else returns false. |
| run_rule_after_enrich | Boolean | Returns true if the rule is configured to run after the enrichment policy, else returns false. |
| exclude_wl | Boolean | Returns true to exclude allowed threat data from passing in the rules, else returns false. |
| exclude_fp | Boolean | Returns true to exclude false-positive threat data from passing in the rules, else returns false. |
| is_follow | Boolean | Returns true if the current user follows the rule, else returns false. |
| ctix_created | Timestamp | Returns the time and date when the rule was created. |
| ctix_modified | Timestamp | Returns the time and date when the rule was last modified. |
| all_sources_and_collections | Boolean | Returns true if all sources are configured on the rule, else returns false. |

| **Parameter** | **Description** |

| component | String: Identifies the CTIX component, for detailed submission to "stix-form" value is returned. |
| enabled_stix_objects | List: specifies the STIX objects user can configure/modify for the current detailed submission. |
| created | Timestamp: created timestamp for the submission |
| modified | Timestamp: modified timestamp for the submission |
| title | String: specifies the title of the stix detailed submission |

post.description:
Adds an Indicator SDO in the initiated detailed STIX form submission.

#### Request Parameters

| **Parameters** | **Type** | **Required** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| confidence | Integer | Optional | Pass the confidence score for the indicator ranging between 0 to 100. |
| created_by_ref | Array | Optional | Pass the list that contains the ID and name of the SDO being referred. |
| revoked | Bool | Optional | Pass true to specify if the SDO is revoked, else pass false. |
| labels | Array | Optional | Pass the list that contains the ID and name of the labels attached to the SDO. |
| name | String | Mandatory | Pass the name of the SDO. |
| description | String | Optional | Pass the description of the SDO. |
| custom_properties | Array | Optional | Pass the list that contains the unique identifier for the custom property, type, name, and the value to add for the same. |
| aliases | Array | Optional | Pass the list of strings that specify the also-known names for the SDO. |
| tlp | Array | Optional | Pass the list that contains the UI labels and value of the TLP. |
| kill_chain_phases | Array | Optional | Pass the list that contains the typical STIX standard kill chain name and the phase name for the same. |
| indicator_types | Array | Optional | Pass the list of STIX vocabulary for indicator types. |
| valid_from | epoch | Mandatory | Pass the date and time since when the SDO is valid. |
| valid_until | epoch | Mandatory | Pass the date and time till which the SDO is valid. |
| observables | Array | Optional | Pass the list that contains parameters, such as expressions and indicator patterns. |
| expressions | Array | Optional | Pass the list containing the STIX pattern expressions. |
| observation_operator | operator | Optional | Pass the operator connecting the comparison expressions. |
| comparison_expression | Array | Optional | Pass the list that specifies the operator and value of the SDO that is part of the expression with meta information like the UI label and the subtype. |
| external_references | Array | Optional | Pass the list that contains the details of the source acting as an external reference. |

#### Response Parameters

| **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| created | epoch | Returns the date and time at which the SDO was created in the application. |
| modified | epoch | Returns the date and time at which the SDO was modified in the application. |
| id | String | Returns the unique identifier of the SDO. |

get.description:
Returns a list of allowed indicators from the CTIX platform.

#### Request Parameters

None

#### Response Parameters

| **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| next | String | Returns a link to the next page. |
| page_size | Integer | Returns the number of records retrieved per page. |
| previous | String | Returns a link to the previous page. |
| total | Integer | Returns the total number of allowed indicators available in the CTIX platform. |
| type | String | Returns the type of the IOC. |
| value | String | Returns the value of the IOC |
| id | String | Returns the unqiue ID of the allowed indicator. |
| modified_by | String | Returns the details of the user who last modified the allowed indicator. |
| modified | Timestamp | Returns the timestamp when the allowed indicator was last modified |
| include_subdomains | Boolean | Returns true if the subdomains of a domain are allowed. Else returns false. |
| include_urls | Boolean | Returns true if the URLs of a domain or IPv4 address are allowed, else returns false. |
| include_emails | Boolean | Returns true if the emails of a domain are allowed, else returns false. |
| created_by | String | Returns the details of the user who created the allowed indicator. |
| created | Timestamp | Returns the timestamp when the allowed indicator was created. |

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Pass the page number to retrieve indicators. Default value: 1

parameters.name: page_size
parameters.description: Pass the number of records to retrieve per page. Default value: 10

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: Pass an allowed indicator type to filter result. To retrieve the supported list of allowed indicator types, se the GET List Supported Allowed Indicator Types API.

parameters.name: created_by_id
parameters.description: Pass the ID of a user to filter records by the creator.

parameters.name: modified_by_id
parameters.description: Pass the ID of a user to filter records by the modifier.

parameters.name: created_from
parameters.description: Pass the created from time in EPOCH format to filter records based on the created time.

parameters.name: created_to
parameters.description: Pass the created to time in EPOCH format to filter records based on the created time.

parameters.name: last_active_from
parameters.description: Pass the last active from time in EPOCH format to filter records based on the last activity time.

parameters.name: last_active_to
parameters.description: Pass the last active to time in EPOCH format to filter records based on the last activity time.

parameters.name: sort
parameters.description: Pass -created to sort result in descending order based on the created time. Pass created to sort result in ascending order.

get.description:
Retrieves a list of keywords from the watchlist.

#### Request Parameters

This API endpoint does not require any input parameter.

#### Response Parameter

| **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| next | String | Returns a link to the next page of the response. |
| page_size | Integer | Returns the number of keywords retrieved. |
| previous | String | Returns a link to the previous page of the response. |
| total | Integer | Returns the total number of keywords available in the watchlist. |
| created | Timestamp | Returns the creation time of the keyword in EPOCH format. |
| created_by | JSON Object | Returns the user details who added the keyword to the watchlist |
| external_recipients | JSON Object | Returns the email addresses of the non-CTIX users who receive the watchlist information. |
| id | String | Returns the ID of the keyword. |
| internal_recipients | JSON Object | Returns the email addresses of the CTIX users who receive the watchlist information. |
| is_active | Boolean | Returns true if the keyword is active, else returns false. |
| is_email_alert | Boolean | Returns true if the email alert is sent for the keyword, else returns false. |
| is_removed | Boolean | Returns true if the keyword is removed, else returns false. |
| last_activity_on | Timestamp | Returns the time in EPOCH format at which the keyword was last active. |
| modified | Timestamp | Returns the time in EPOCH format at which the keyword was last modified. |
| name | String | Returns the value of the keyword. |
| occurrences | Integer | Returns the number of occurrences a keyword is observed in the platform. |
| updated_by | JSON Object | Returns the user details who last updated the keyword. |

parameters.description: Pass the last active to time in EPOCH format.

| name | String | Mandatory | Pass the value of the keyword. |
| is_active | Boolean | Optional | Pass true to activate the keyword, else pass false to deactivate. |
| is_removed | Boolean | Optional | Pass true to remove the keyword, else pass false. |

#### Response Parameters

| **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| created | Timestamp | Returns the date and time of the creation of a watchlist keyword. |
| created_by | String | Returns the ID of the user that created the watchlist keyword. |
| external_recipients | JSON | Returns the list of email addresses of the external recipients. |
| id | String | Returns the ID of the watchlist keyword. |
| internal_recipients | JSON | Returns the user details of the internal recipients. |
| is_active | Boolean | Returns true if the watchlist keyword is active, else returns false. |
| is_email_alert | Boolean | Returns true if an email alert is sent for the watchlist keyword, else returns false. |
| is_removed | Boolean | Returns true if the watchlist keyword is removed, else returns false. |
| last_activity_on | Timestamp | Returns the date and time at which the watchlist keyword was last active. |
| modified | Timestamp | Returns the date and time of modification of the watchlist keyword. |
| name | String | Returns the value of the watchlist keyword. |
| occurrences | Integer | Returns the number of occurrences found for a watchlist keyword. |
| updated_by | String | Returns the ID of the user that updated the watchlist keyword. |

get.description:
Retrieves a list of reports from the CTIX application. #### Request Parameters This API endpoint does not require any request parameter. #### Response Parameters | **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** | | --- | --- | --- | | next | String | Returns the URL link to the next page. | | previous | String | Returns the URL link of the previous page. | | page_size | Integer | Returns the size of the requested page. | | results | JSON Object | Returns results in JSON objects for each report. | | schedule | JSON Object | Returns the details of the scheduled runs. | | schedule: id | String | Returns the unique ID of the schedule. | | schedule: repeat_type | String | Returns the time interval for the re-run of the report. | | schedule: start_datetime | Timestamp | Returns the starting date and time in EPOCH format from which the report captures the received data. | | schedule: repeat_value | Integer | Returns the number of times a report has to run during the provided interval. | | schedule: ends_on | JSON Object | Returns the end type, number of attempts left, and end time. | | schedule: duration_value | Integer | Returns the duration in days to capture data in the report. | | query_key | String | Returns the sorting type in basic reports:
`ctix_created`: Data in the report is sorted based on the system created date.
`ctix_modified`: Data in the report is sorted based on the system modified date. | | name | String | Returns the title of the report. | | id | String | Returns the unique ID of the report. | | columns | Array of JSON objects | Returns the list of columns in a basic report. | | internal_recipients | JSON Object | Returns the list of internal recipients with whom to share the report. | | external_recipients | JSON Object | Returns the list of external recipients with whom to share the report. | | type | String | Returns the type of report, such as a basic or advanced. | | file_types | Array | Returns the report format, such as CSV and XLS for basic reports and PDF for advanced reports. | | shared_type | String | Returns the shared type, such as global or private. | | date_last_run | Timestamp | Returns the date and time in EPOCH format at which the report was the last run. | | created_by | JSON Object | Returns the details of the user who created the report. | | modified_by | JSON Object | Returns the details of the user who last modified the report. |

post.description:
Creates a basic (standard) report in CTIX. > Do not use this endpoint to create advanced reports. #### Request Parameters | **Parameters** | **Type** | **Required** | **Description** | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | schedule | JSON Object | Optional | Pass the schedule details such as repeat type, start date and time, duration time, duration value, and repeat value. | | schedule: start_datetime | Timestamp | Mandatory | Enter the start time of the report in EPOCH format. | | schedule: repeat_type | String | Mandatory | Pass the frequency of the schedule.
Allowed values:
`once daily weekly monthly` | | schedule: ends_on | JSON Object | Mandatory | Pass the end time of the report. | | schedule: ends_on: type | String | Mandatory | Pass the schedule end type.
Allowed values:
`never end_date occurrences` | | schedule: ends_on: value | Timestamp (If the `ends_on: type` is `end_date`)
Integer (If the `ends_on: type` is `occurences`) | Mandatory | Pass the end time in EPOCH format or the number of occurences. | | schedule: duration_value | Integer | Mandatory | Pass the time frame in days to include the data in the report. | | saved_search | JSON Object | Mandatory | Pass the saved search or custom query details used for generating the report. | | saved_search: id
(To create a report using a saved search) | String | Mandatory | Pass the ID of a saved search to generate the report. | | saved_search: query
(To create a report using custom filters) | String | Mandatory | Pass the query to generate the report. | | saved_search: meta_data
(To create a report using custom filters) | String | Optional | Pass any metadata to include. | | query_key | String | Optional | Pass the key to sort data in the report:
`ctix_created` (Default): Data in the report is sorted based on the system created date.
`ctix_modified`: Data in the report is sorted based on the system modified date. | | name | String | Mandatory | Pass the name of the report. | | columns | Array of JSON objects | Mandatory | Pass the list of columns to be displayed in a basic report. | | file_types | Array | Mandatory | Pass the report type, such as `csv`, `xls`. | | type | String | Mandatory | Pass the type of report to be created, such as standard or advanced.
Allowed value:
`basic` (to create standard reports) | | shared_type | String | Mandatory | Pass the share type value.
Allowed values:
`private`
`global` | | internal_recipients | JSON Object | Optional | Pass the list of internal recipients with whom to share the report. | | external_recipients | JSON Object | Optional | Pass the list of external recipients with whom to share the report. | #### Response Parameters | **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** | | --- | --- | --- | | basic_report_type | String | Returns the type of basic report, such as saved or custom. | | schedule: id | String | Returns the unique ID of the schedule. | | schedule: repeat_type | String | Returns the time interval for the re-run of the report. | | schedule: start_datetime | Timestamp | Returns the starting date and time in EPOCH format from which the report captures the received data. | | schedule: repeat_value | Integer | Returns the schedule frequency. | | schedule: duration_type | String | Returns the end duration interval for the captured data. | | schedule: duration_value | Integer | Returns the duration in days to capture data in the report. | | query_key | String | Returns the sorting type in basic reports:
`ctix_created`: Data in the report is sorted based on the system created date.
`ctix_modified`: Data in the report is sorted based on the system modified date. | | name | String | Returns the name of the report. | | id | String | Returns the unique ID of the report. | | columns | Array of JSON objects | Returns the list of columns in a report. | | internal_recipients | JSON Object | Returns the list of internal recipients with whom to share the report. | | external_recipients | JSON Object | Returns the list of external recipients with whom to share the report. | | type | String | Returns the type of report, such as basic or advanced. | | file_types | Array | Returns the report formats, such as CSV, XLS. | | shared_type | String | Returns the shared type, such as global or private. | | date_last_run | Timestamp | Returns the date and time in EPOCH format at which the report was manually run. | | schedule_last_run | Timestamp | Returns the scheduled date and time in EPOCH fromat of the report generation. | | saved_search | JSON Object | Returns the saved search object for the corresponding report used for generating the report. | | created_by | JSON Object | Returns the details of the user who created the report. |

`ctix_created`: Data in the report is sorted based on the system created date.
`ctix_modified`: Data in the report is sorted based on the system modified date. | | name | String | Returns the name of the report. | | id | String | Returns the unique ID of the report. | | columns | Array of JSON Objects | Returns the list of columns in a report. | | internal_recipients | JSON Object | Returns the list of internal recipients with whom to share the report. | | external_recipients | JSON Object | Returns the list of external recipients with whom to share the report. | | type | String | Returns the type of report, such as basic or advanced. | | file_types | Array | Returns the report formats, such as CSV, XLS. | | shared_type | String | Returns the shared type, such as global or private. | | date_last_run | Timestamp | Returns the date and time in EPOCH format at which the report was manually run. | | schedule_last_run | Timestamp | Returns the scheduled date and time in EPOCH format of the report generation. | | saved_search | JSON Object | Returns the saved search object for the corresponding report used for generating the report. | | created_by | JSON Object | Returns the details of the user who created the report. |

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: [Mandatory] Pass the type of report to be created. Allowed value: basic

delete.description:
Deletes a report from the CTIX application.

#### Request Parameters

This API does not require any request parameter.

#### Response Parameters

Status code 204: The report id deleted successfully.

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: [Mandatory] Pass the type of report to be deleted. Allowed value: basic, advanced

parameters.name: report_id
parameters.description: Pass the ID of a report to delete(87c07bb3-b60c-4279-a5ec-8a38f73f7ea4)

get.description:
Retrieves the run logs of a report.

#### Request Parameters

This API endpoint does not require any request parameter.

#### Response Parameters

| **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| total | Integer | Returns the total number of run logs available for a report. |
| next | String | Returns the URL of the next page. |
| previous | String | Returns the URL of the previous page. |
| files | Array of JSON Object | Returns the file object that contains meta-information for generating the downloadable link of the report. |
| files: id | String | Returns the unique ID of the file. |
| files: type | String | Returns the type of file such as csv or xlsx. |
| files: url | String | Returns the download URL of the file. |
| files: name | String | Returns the name of the report. |
| id | String | Returns the unique ID of the run log. |
| run_type | String | Returns the run-type applied on the report, such as scheduled, instant. |
| last_run | epoch | Returns the date and time at which the file was last run. |
| file_types | Array | Returns the list of file types in a run log, such as CSV, and XLS. |
| download_link | JSON Object | Returns the URL to download the report. |

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: [Mandatory] Pass the type of report to retrieve run logs. Allowed value: basic, advanced

parameters.name: page_size
parameters.description: Pass the number of records to retrieve per page.

post.description:
Runs a report and sends the file to the recipients. #### Request Parameters | **Parameter** | **Type** | **Required** | **Description** | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | file_types | Array | Optional | Pass the report types. Allowed values:
`csv`
`xls` | | internal_recipients | JSON Object | Optional
(At least one internal or external recipient is required) | Pass the list of details of internal users to share the report. | | internal_recipients: to | Array | Optional | Pass the details of an internal user to share the report. | | internal_recipients: to: first_name | String | Optional | Pass the first name of the internal recipient. | | internal_recipients: to: last_name | String | Optional | Pass the last name of the internal recipient. | | internal_recipients: to: email | String | Optional | Pass the email address of the internal recipient. | | internal_recipients: to: id | String | Optional | Pass the unique identifier of the internal recipient. | | query_key | String | Mandatory | Pass the value to specify whether the report must run based on the created time or last modified time of threat data in CTIX. Allowed values are ctix_created and ctix_modified. | | starttime | Timestamp | Mandatory | Pass the start date and time in EPOCH format from which the report captures data available in CTIX. | | endtime | Timestamp | Mandatory | Pass the end date and time in EPOCH format until which the report captures data available in CTIX. | | external_recipients | JSON Object | Optional
(At least one internal or external recipient is required) | Pass the list of external users to share the report. | | external_recipients: to | Array | Optional | Pass the email addresses of the external recipients. | #### Response Parameters | **Parameter** | **Type** | **Description** | | --- | --- | --- | | result | String | Returns the following success message:
"Your Report will be mailed to you as soon as it is ready." |

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: [Mandatory] Pass the type of report to run. Allowed value: basic, advanced

result.example: Your Report will be mailed to you as soon as it is ready.

value.result: Your Report will be mailed to you as soon as it is ready.

parameters.name: report_id
parameters.description: Pass the ID of a report (87c07bb3-b60c-4279-a5ec-8a38f73f7ea4)

tags.name: Ping
tags.description: CTIX enables you to check the connectivity with a server through the Ping Pong method. The Ping Pong method sends a request called ping to check the connection and a successful response is called a pong response.

tags.name: Administration
tags.description: Learn about the APIs to manage the administrative features such as integrations, configurations, usermanagement, and more.

tags.name: Integration Management
tags.description: Learn about the APIs to manage various types of integrations, such as threat intel feed sources, third-party and Cyware tool integrations, browser extensions, and more.

tags.name: STIX Source
tags.description: Structured Threat Information Expression (STIX) sources deliver threat intel in CTIX in the STIX format. The supported STIX formats are STIX 1.x, STIX 2.0, and STIX 2.1. Use the APIs to retrieve details about STIX sources and collections.

tags.name: API Integrations
tags.description:
Application Programming Interface (API) feeds are software intermediaries that allow two applications to communicate with each other. In Intel Exchange, administrators can configure API feed sources to receive threat intelligence data at specified time intervals. Each API feed source provides unique feed channels to receive different types of threat intelligence data, such as hashes, URLs, indicators, threat actors, and more feeds from various sources. You can manage the API connector configurations and start receiving threat intel packages from the configured API sources.

This section contains APIs related to the API feed sources in the integration management module.

tags.name: RSS Feeds
tags.description: This folder contains APIs for RSS feeds.

tags.name: WebScraper
tags.description: Webscrappers can be used to get large amounts of data from websites. You can configure webscrappers in CTIX. Use the APIs in this module to look for information realted to webscrappers configured in CTIX.

tags.name: Webhooks
tags.description: A webhook delivers data to an application as it happens. You can configure webhook URLs in CTIX. Use the APIs in this module to look for information realted to webhooks configured in CTIX.

tags.name: Source Weightage
tags.description: CTIX assigns source weightage to intel source based on the accuracy of data received from it during the past.

tags.name: Tool Integrations
tags.description:
Configure Tool Integrations under Integration Management in CTIX to do the following:

*   Communicate with other Cyware products, such as CSAP and CSOL.
*   Configure integrations with third-party applications, such as SIEM, SOAR, and more.
*   Send information from CTIX to other applications.
*   Perform actions on third-party applications, such as SIEM, SOAR, and more.

tags.name: Tool Details
tags.description: This API determines the integrated tool types, categories, and sub-categories defined in CTIX.

tags.name: Tool Accounts
tags.description: All the tools added in the CTIX application can have multiple accounts. You can find the APIs related to the different accounts of the third-party integration tools in this section.

tags.name: Actions
tags.description: In CTIX, you can perform different actions on the configured accounts of your third-party integrated applications. Some of the actions are polling for IOCs, defining a quota for enrichment tools, and more.

tags.name: Subscriber Management
tags.description: Susbcribers are members or entities in CTIX application with whom you can share intel and information. Use the APIs in this folder to get details about the CTIX subscribers.

tags.name: Subscriber Weightage
tags.description:
The CTIX Confidence Score engine calculates the Confidence Score of the intel received from various sources by a weighted average combination of the four parameters namely Source Sightings, Relations, Enrichment Policy, and Source Confidence.

You can assign the source Confidence Score (subscriber weightage) to the subscribers based on the accuracy of data received from them during the past.

tags.name: Subscriber Report
tags.description: The subscriber reports include polling reports and inboxing reports that provide an overview of the threat data sharing activity across subscribers.

tags.name: Users and Groups
tags.description: Use APIs in this module to create, update, or view details of users and user groups in CTIX.

tags.name: Users
tags.description: Users allow you to manage the CTIX Users and their permissions. You can assign each user a unique identity, with the necessary access permissions in CTIX application.

tags.name: User Groups
tags.description: A user group is a collection of CTIX users segregated based on their access permissions across the application. User Group allows you to give feature specific or action specific permissions to users.

tags.name: Custom Entitites Management
tags.description: Custom entities management includes custom components such as custom objects, custom attributes, and custom kill chain phases. Custom entities helps you to characterize threat data objects, attributes, and kill chain phases that are not defined by standard STIX schema or the standard Kill Chain phases.

tags.name: Custom Attributes
tags.description: Custom attributes helps you to add additional information to indicators that are not defined by STIX. Some examples of Custom Attributes include Credit Card number, CVV Number, and Expiry Date.

tags.name: Custom Objects
tags.description: Custom Objects are custom STIX objects that provide additional context to any threat intel. Custom Objects characterize threat data components that are not described by STIX components. The application allows you to create custom objects that can be mapped with custom attributes and are used while creating custom intel packages in the application. Custom objects can be organization- or sector-specific. Some examples include Payment Card information, IMEI numbers, SIM Card number, and so on.

tags.name: Custom Kill Chain
tags.description:
The Cyber Kill Chain is a cybersecurity model created by Lockheed Martin that traces the stages of an attack, identifies vulnerabilities, and helps security teams to stop the attacks at every stage of the chain.

MITRE ATT&CK also defines the stages of an attack and helps teams to come up with mitigation tactics and techniques.

Apart from using these standard Kill Chains, you can create your own kill chain with its own custom phases suitable for your organization and industry. Create, maintain, and map the custom kill chains when adding intel in CTIX using Detailed Intel Submission.

tags.name: Enrichment Management
tags.description:
CTIX enriches the threat data by removing false positives, adding contextual information, and scoring indicators by identifying key malicious properties.

CTIX has integrations defined with many enrichment tools. Analysts can perform enrichment operations either manually or by configuring an enrichment policy with the help of third-party tool integrations.

tags.name: Enrichment Policy
tags.description: An enrichment policy automatically enriches the threat intel data coming into the CTIX application.

tags.name: Enrichment Tools
tags.description: Use APIs in this module to get the details about the enrichment tools configured in CTIX.

tags.name: Enrichment Details
tags.description: Use the APIs in this module to view enrichment details for threat data objects. You can fetch the details such as enrichment status, verdict of the enrichment, tools used for enrichment and more.

tags.name: Audit Log Management
tags.description: Audit logs help administrators monitor the activities in the platform and ensure that users follow the defined protocols of an organization.

tags.name: License Management System
tags.description: CTIX leverages the capabilities of the License Management System (LMS) and allows its tenants to utilize them. This API retrieves information about the tenant license.

tags.name: Certificate
tags.description:
CTIX allows you to create and manage certificates used for authentication of various sources configured for receiving threat intel. These certificates are used as an authentication type for adding intel sources that communicate using the TAXII URL functionality.

This module contains the API to retrieve certificates.

tags.name: Configuration
tags.description: CTIX enables you to configure various configuration types, such as tenant, authentication, component, SAML, and more.

tags.name: Tenant Configuration
tags.description: A tenant configuration involves setting up both sides of the network, the server side and the spine side of the network.

tags.name: Rate Limit
tags.description: Rate limit defines the amount of data you receive in a specified period.

tags.name: Ingestion Service Config
tags.description: Retrieve configuration of the ingestion service.

tags.name: TAXII Server Configuration
tags.description: Use the APIs in this module to retrieve the TAXII server configuration details of the platform.

tags.name: System Notifications
tags.description: You can configure the system notifications of the platform to send timely in-app and email notifications to relevant users about errors related to feed sources, subscribers, rules, and API credentials. The notifications enable the users to take proactive measures and avoid any disruption in the platform.

tags.name: Custom Scores
tags.description: Custom scores for threat data objects provide you the flexibility to define scores based on the parameters that impact the lifecycle of indicators of compromise (IOCs), such as relevance, severity, risk, and more.

tags.name: Collection Management
tags.description: Collections are groupings in CTIX which contain intel collected from different sources. The collections categorize threat intel and help you differntiate information while sharing them with subscribers.

tags.name: Tag Management
tags.description: Tags are labels or keywords used to categorize and organize threat intel. They help classify and group related data, making it easier to search, filter, and manage the information within the platform. From user-defined tags to source, system, and privileged access tags, this provides a seamless platform to organize and administer tag categories.

tags.name: Quick Add Intel
tags.description:
Quick Add Intel enables you to quickly add intel into CTIX by providing some basic details.

This section describes how to use the API endpoints related to Quick Add Intel.

tags.name: Import Intel
tags.description: You can import intel from structured data sources such as STIX bundles, CSV files, and OpenIOC into ​Intel Exchange (CTIX)​​ and use these imported sources to create intel within the platform.

tags.name: Create Intel via Open API
tags.description: Create intel with additional details such as the source and collection to better group and correlate data.

tags.name: Ingest Bundle
tags.description:
The Ingest Bundle endpoint allows you to import threat intelligence data into Intel Exchange in the STIX format. Using this endpoint, you can directly ingest STIX bundles from external sources into collections for further enrichment and analysis.

You can ingest bundles in two ways:

- With Source and Collection Name: Ingest data by specifying the STIX source and collection name.
    
- With Source and Collection ID: Ingest data by referencing the unique collection ID linked to a STIX source.

tags.name: Threat Data
tags.description: Intel received in CTIX from different sources is categorized into various STIX object types, normalized, and deduplicated for further analysis. Threat Data module displays all the ingested objects for analysis and actioning.

tags.name: Export
tags.description: Export threat data objects from the CTIX application into a CSV file for offline analysis.

tags.name: Bulk Actions
tags.description: Perform an action on multiple threat data objects with one API request.

tags.name: Quick Preview
tags.description: Use quick preview in Threat Data to retrieve specific details of a threat data object.

tags.name: Miscellaneous
tags.description:
This section includes the APIs to retrieve IOC types, country details, source types, sources, and collections of the sources.

# Supported SDO Types

CTIX supports the following SDO types in Threat Data.

| SDO Type | SDO Key |
| --- | --- |
| Vulnerability | `vulnerability` |
| Malware | `malware` |
| Campaign | `campaign` |
| Threat Actor | `threat-actor` |
| Intrusion Set | `intrusion-set` |
| Attack Pattern | `attack-pattern` |
| Incident | `incident` |
| Course of Action | `course-of-action` |
| Identity | `identity` |
| Tool | `tool` |
| Infrastructure | `infrastructure` |
| Location | `location` |
| Malware Analysis | `malware-analysis` |
| Custom Objects | `custom-object` |
| Indicator | `indicator` |
| Note | `note` |
| Observed Data | `observed-data` |
| Opinion | `opinion` |
| Report | `report` |
| Observables | `observable` |

tags.name: Saved Search
tags.description: Save the frequently searched threat data elements and CQL queries. You can share a saved search with other users for use.

tags.name: Threat Data Objects
tags.description: CTIX segregates the different formats of threat intel received into STIX Objects such as Indicators, Vulnerabilities, TTPs, Malware, Campaign, Threat Actor, Intrusion Set, Attack Pattern, Incident, Course of Action, Identity, Kill Chain, Kill Chain Phases, and Tool.

tags.name: Basic Details
tags.description: Retrieves details about the threat data objects in CTIX.

tags.name: Confidence Score
tags.description: A confidence score is a value between zero and 100 assigned automatically to threat indicators and represents the confidence that the scoring engine has in that indicator being malicious.

tags.name: Custom Attributes1
tags.description: Custom Attributes provide additional information that enhances the details of threat intelligence. These attributes are not limited to specific types or categories and can be customized based on the unique requirements of security and business operations.

tags.name: Relations
tags.description: The Relations section in the Threat Data module provides comprehensive information on the relationship details of a threat data object.

tags.name: Enrichment
tags.description: CTIX offers you to enrich a threat data object directly from the Threat Data module. This section contains the API related to enriching a threat data object from the Threat Data module.

tags.name: Actions1
tags.description: Actions module retrieves the details about the actions performed on a threat data object.

tags.name: Tasks
tags.description: Tasks are the actions taken by analysts to respond to threats for containment, proactive prevention, or remediation. You can create tasks in the individual modules, individual threat data elements, and in the global task module as well.

tags.name: Notes
tags.description: Notes include additional information of a threat data object that the analysts want to share with internal teams and tenants. Using notes, you can share indicators, threat intel data, threat data details, actionable tasks, or any other details.

tags.name: Quick Action
tags.description: CTIX offers to perform various quick actions, such as deprecating, marking false-positive, reviewing, revoking, and more on a threat data element. This module contains APIs related to quick actions.

tags.name: Threat Mailbox
tags.description: CTIX offers Threat Mailbox to receive threat intel feeds from multiple email accounts. CTIX fetches intel from the attachments, email content, and password protected attachments.

tags.name: Threat Bulletin
tags.description: Threat Bulletin is a summary of recent activities, emerging trends, or research analysis data that can help your organization to keep pace with the evolving cyber threat landscape.

tags.name: RSS Feeds1
tags.description: Rich Site Summary (RSS) web feeds contains a summary of updates from a website, often in the form of a list of articles with links, in a computer-readable format. These feeds help you stay up-to-date about threat intelligence updates.

tags.name: Global Notes
tags.description: Notes include additional information of a threat data object that the analysts want to share with internal teams and tenants. Using notes, you can share indicators, threat intel data, threat data details, actionable tasks, or any other details.

tags.name: Threat Investigation
tags.description: Threat Investigations in CTIX helps you to investigate security investigations with improved insights on a visual canvas. You can use the interactive graphical visualization canvas to analyze threat data.

tags.name: Threat Data1
tags.description: This section includes the APIs to retrieve threat data object details to use in threat investigations.

tags.name: Enrichment1
tags.description: Use the API in this section to get perform enrichment on threat data from the threat investigations module.

tags.name: Relationship
tags.description: Use the API in this section to gain an understanding of the relationship types between any two STIX 2.1 objects or two threat data objects.

tags.name: Publishing
tags.description: Use the APIs in this section to publish the intel, create a draft, and view intel publishing history in threat investigations.

tags.name: Canvas
tags.description: This section contains APIs related to the threat investigation canvas

tags.name: Sandbox
tags.description: CTIX offers Sandbox capability that helps analysts to perform deep analysis of evasive and unknown threats. Sandbox is a testing environment where analysts can execute potentially malicious files or URL requests in an isolated area away from an organization's network. Files are executed and tested without any threat to computers or networks.

tags.name: Threat Defender Library
tags.description: Threat Defender Library acts as a central repository to contain information and files used in threat detection, hunting, and threat defense. The unique content in this repository adds value to the existing threat-hunting and detection workflows by allowing analysts to quickly respond to organization-specific threats.

tags.name: ATT&CK Navigator
tags.description: The MITRE ATT&CK Navigator is a web-based tool for annotating and exploring ATT&CK matrices. It can be used to visualize defensive coverage, red/blue team planning, the frequency of detected techniques, and more. Navigator tactics include various strategies used by the threat actors whereas techniques define the way of achieving the strategies.

tags.name: ATT&CK Layer
tags.description: The CTIX ATT&CK Navigator enables you to clone and manage multiple layers based on the **MITRE** or **Custom Base Layer**. The layers constitute tactics and techniques used to track and monitor specific adversaries and platforms. You can browse, select, and add the set of techniques and custom techniques to match the particular criteria for specific projects.

tags.name: Techniques
tags.description: ATT&CK Navigator techniques define the way of achieving the strategies. This section includes APIs to manage ATT&CK Navigator techniques.

tags.name: Fang/Defang
tags.description: Use Fang-Defang to neutralize malicious information with an obfuscated representation so that it is not dangerous if this information is inadvertently clicked or automatically processed in error.

tags.name: Rules
tags.description: Rules are a predefined set of instructions that can perform automated tasks when the defined situation or condition occurs.

tags.name: Save Result Set
tags.description:
Save Result Set is a specification in CTIX that is designed to make CTIX data available over a web service such as OpenAPI.

Use Rules on CTIX to make indicators available over the Save Result Set.

tags.name: Global Tasks
tags.description: Tasks are the actions taken by analysts to respond to threats for containment, proactive prevention, or remediation. You can create tasks in the individual modules, individual threat data elements, and in the global task module as well.

tags.name: Detailed Submission
tags.description: Analysts can create detailed threat intel and publish intel to their subscribers in CTIX by submitting detailed information for the supported STIX objects.

tags.name: Common
tags.description: Folder contains the common APIs that the stix form component uses to serve static data.

tags.name: DRAFT
tags.description: Contains the API calls for creating intel in DRAFT state. The following API calls in the folder are the first interaction the users have when creating intel using the STIX Forms.

tags.name: Attack Pattern
tags.description: Attack patterns are a type of Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTP) that describes ways that adversaries attempt to compromise targets.

tags.name: SCOs
tags.description: SCO defines a set of one or more properties named **ID Contributing Properties**. These properties may be used in the default calculation of the ID when creating an SCO. In some cases, an additional selection of extension properties that contribute to the ID may be described in the **ID Contributing Properties** section listed on each SCO.

tags.name: Relationship1
tags.description: The Relationship object is used to link together two SDOs or SCOs in order to describe how they are related to each other. If SDOs and SCOs are considered nodes or vertices in the graph, the Relationship Objects (SROs) represent edges.

tags.name: Sighting
tags.description: A Sighting denotes the belief that something in CTI (e.g., an indicator, malware, tool, threat actor, etc.) was seen. Sightings are used to track who and what are being targeted, how attacks are carried out, and to track trends in attack behavior.

tags.name: Campaign
tags.description: A Campaign is a grouping of adversarial behaviors that describes a set of malicious activities or attacks (sometimes called waves) that occur over a period of time against a specific set of targets. Campaigns usually have well defined objectives and may be part of an Intrusion Set.

tags.name: Course Of Action
tags.description:
A Course of Action is an action taken either to prevent an attack or to respond to an attack that is in progress. It may describe technical, automatable responses (applying patches, reconfiguring firewalls) but can also describe higher -evel actions like employee training or policy changes. For example, a course of action to mitigate a vulnerability could describe applying the patch that fixes it.

The Course of Action SDO contains a textual description of the action; a reserved action property also serves as a placeholder for future inclusion of machine automatable courses of action.

tags.name: Identity
tags.description: The Identity SDO can capture basic identifying information, contact information, and the sectors that the Identity belongs. Identity is used in STIX to represent, among other things, targets of attacks, information sources, object creators, and threat actor identities.

tags.name: Location
tags.description: The Location SDO is related to an Identity or Intrusion Set to indicate that the identity or intrusion set is located in that location. It is also related to a malware or attack pattern to indicate that they target victims in that location. The Location object describes geographic areas, not governments, even in cases where that area might have a government. For example, a Location representing the United States describes the United States as a geographic area, not the federal government of the United States.

tags.name: Infrastructure
tags.description: Infrastructure refers to the physical components of interrelated systems providing commodities and services essential to enable, sustain, or enhance societal living conditions. Infrastructure can be fundamental facilities and systems serving a country, city, or other areas, including the services and facilities necessary for its economy to function. Infrastructure can also comprise public and private physical improvements such as roads, railways, bridges, tunnels, water supply, sewers, electrical grids, and telecommunications (including Internet connectivity and broadband speeds).

tags.name: Threat Actor
tags.description:
Threat actors are actual individuals, groups, or organizations that operate with malicious intent.

Threat actors can be characterized by their motives, capabilities, goals, sophistication level, past activities, resources they have access to, and their role in the organization.

tags.name: Tool
tags.description: The Tool threat data object characterizes the properties of these software tools and can be used as a basis for asserting how a threat actor uses them during an attack. It contains properties to name and describe the tool, a list of kill chain phases the tool is used to carry out, and the version of the tool.

tags.name: Vulnerability
tags.description: A vulnerability is a weakness or a defect in the requirements, designs, or implementations of the computational logic found in software and some hardware components that can be directly exploited to negatively impact the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of that system.

tags.name: Report
tags.description: Reports are collections of threat intelligence that may include descriptions of a threat actor, malware, or attack technique, including context and related details. They are used to group related threat intel items tied together so that they can be published as a comprehensive cyber threat story. The report component contains a list of references to STIX Objects (the CTI objects included in the report) along with a textual description and the name of the report.

tags.name: Failure Logs
tags.description: Analysts can view logs with details of any failures that may occur while submitting intel in CTIX using a threat bulletin or detailed submission form. This helps analysts track their intel submissions and troubleshoot issues.

tags.name: Indicators Allowed
tags.description: The allowed list consists of indicators that are considered non-malicious for the platform. This set of APIs are used to create, retrieve, update, and delete an allowed list.

tags.name: Third-Party Indicators
tags.description: CTIX now supports accessing third-party repositories, such as Majestic Million, which provides a list of well-known IOCs.With access to millions of IOCs, CTIX can verify incoming data, enhancing the detection and segregation of well-known IOCs. These repositories also help filter commonly known allowed indicators, allowing focused research on relevant and targeted intelligence. Additionally, if an allowed indicator is reported as suspicious, CTIX offers an option to add it to the third-party ignored list.

tags.name: Watchlist
tags.description: A Watchlist is any keyword that you can add for surveillance or to closely monitor. It can be an IP address, a hash, a domain, a URL, a technical term, a non-technical term, or any other word or number.

tags.name: Tags
tags.description: Tags are the labels attached to threat data objects to identify or group them.

tags.name: Status Check
tags.description: Checks the service status of your subscribers and collections.

tags.name: Dashboards
tags.description: Dashboards allow you to visualize data that impact the security efforts on the overall Threat Intelligence lifecycle. You can gain a better understanding of the threats your organization faces. Analysts from the threat intelligence teams can monitor the flow of threat intelligence data in an actionable and meaningful way.

tags.name: Widgets
tags.description:
Widgets are the building blocks of a dashboard that consolidate the related data of one type and provide valuable information. A dashboard includes multiple widgets that are related to the use case of the dashboard. There are two types of widgets:

- **Summary Widgets**: Summary widgets display information about a specific entity based on the time frame selected for the dashboard. For example, Domain Objects.
- **Information Widgets**: Information widgets display a comparison among two or more entities based on the time frame selected for the dashboard. You can view data in various layouts, such as graphs, pie charts, line charts, and more. For example, Feed Sources vs IOCs.

tags.name: Reports
tags.description:
Reports present complex data and insights in a more understandable and actionable format. Reports further empower analysts to share timely and relevant information with key stakeholders, such as management, security teams, and other relevant parties.

CTIX supports the following types of reports:

- **Standard Reports**: Leverage saved searches, CQL queries, and filters to access specific data sets and build reports based on the selected parameters.
- **Advanced Reports**: Leverage widgets, tables, queries, filters, and images to access data and present them in an understandable and visually appealing format.
